GB1-W07-A1 - Distance Mgmt + Front Push.body:
## Concept

**Position:** Standing / Distance Management
**Category:** Takedown / Self-Defense
**Curriculum:** GB1 Week 7 A1
**Confidence:** 4/5

From distance, use a front push kick to manage range and create entry angles, then secure a body lock (waist or high clinch) and drive the takedown (bodylock or double leg variations with turning the corner or inside hook trip) directly into side mount control. This is a core GB1 Week 7 self-defense and sport sequence emphasizing distance management as the foundation for safe, high-percentage entries.

## Setup

You are standing at striking/kicking distance. Opponent is postured or advancing.

- Goal: Use push kick to disrupt balance/timing, close distance under control, secure body lock with strong posture and wrist/hip control, and finish the takedown (bodylock drive or double leg turn/inside hook) landing in dominant side mount.
- Direct follow-up to prior self-defense takedown work (W03-A1 jab slip double leg, W04-A1 outside hook, W02 turtle double leg) and connects forward to W07 guard passing and W08 mount transitions/attacks.

Wikilink connections:
- Part of Week 7 distance + takedown family: see [[GB1-W07-B1 - Distance Mgmt + Jab + Cross + Double Leg]], [[GB1-W08-B1 - Jab Slip + Bodylock Clinch + Rear Bodylock + Wrist Control + Forward Takedown]].
- Builds on [[GB1-W03-A1 - Jab Slip to Double Leg Takedown]] and [[GB1-W04-A1 - Double Lapel Grab Escape + Outside Hook Takedown + Straight Armbar]] for consistent level change, hip drive, and head position mechanics.
- Cross-ref to [[GB1-W02-B3 - Double Leg Takedown from Turtle Position]] and W06 turtle recoveries for entry/exit flow.
- Leads into W07 guard passes (A2/A3) and W08 mount work (A2/B2) once side mount is secured.

## Execution

### Push Kick for Distance Management + Entry Setup
1. Maintain upright posture and active hands at distance.
2. Front push kick (teep) to the hip or midsection to freeze or push opponent back, creating a momentary timing window and disrupting their base.
3. Immediately close the gap behind the kick with level change (hips back, knees bent, not shoulders dropping first).

### Body Lock Entry + Control (Wrist/Hip/Weight)
1. Secure the body lock (high clinch or waist wrap) with inside wrist control or collar tie on the far side.
2. Keep head position up/outside or tight to their shoulder/hip line to avoid counters; chest to chest or hip to hip pressure.
3. Drive with hip elevation and forward pressure (weight through your base/legs, not floating on toes). Use the body lock grip to lift or turn their hips off base.

### Takedown Finish (Body Lock Drive or Double Leg Turn/Inside Hook)
1. For body lock: continue the drive, elevate their hips or trip the near leg while maintaining the lock and base.
2. For double leg variations (per curriculum): turn the corner (angle your hips 45-90 degrees) or use inside hook trip on their posting leg as you drive through.
3. Land in side mount with heavy base, one knee in hip, far hip/thigh frame or underhook, head control or posture up to prevent immediate guard recovery or bridge.

### Hand Placement & References
- **Push kick target (hip/midsection)**: Creates the freeze and entry angle without overcommitting.
- **Body lock grip (waist or high clinch + inside wrist/collar tie)**: The control that allows hip lift and turn. Wrist control prevents their underhook or frame.
- **Level change (hips back, knees bent, head up/outside)**: The entry motor — not a shoulder dip that exposes the neck (consistent with W03-A1 Harrogate/Scottsdale mechanics).
- **Hip drive + weight through base/legs during finish**: Forward pressure + slight elevation to off-balance; inside hook or corner turn for the leg trip (GB Centennial Week 7).
- **Side mount arrival (knee in hip, far hip frame, head/posture)**: Immediate dominant control, not a loose landing that lets them recover guard.

## Common Mistakes
- Kicking too high or without follow-up close (opponent recovers posture and times a counter).
- Dropping level with shoulders first (exposes neck/head to guillotine or sprawl).
- Weak body lock (arms only, no chest/hip pressure) — they frame or underhook and separate.
- No corner/angle on double leg finish (straight drive gets stuffed or they spin out).
- Landing side mount with floating weight or no immediate frame (they bridge or recover half guard easily).
- Losing head position during the drive (choke or armbar threats).

## Key Principles
- **Distance management + body lock takedown for securing side control (GB Centennial Week 7 curriculum)**: The explicit GB1 Week 7 focus — use push kick or range tools to set up the body lock entry, then drive to side mount. "Master the body lock takedown for securing side control."
- **Double leg turning the corner + inside hook trip (GB Centennial Week 7)**: Not a straight blast double; angle your hips ("turn the corner") or hook the inside leg as you change levels and drive. Creates the high-percentage finish even against good sprawl.
- **Level change + hip drive with head up/outside (consistent GB Harrogate/Scottsdale + W03-A1)**: Hips back and knees bent for entry (not shoulders); head position protects and maintains posture. "Level change + hip drive (not shoulders); head up/outside during drive; heavy top + arm feed finish."
- **Wrist control + chest/hip pressure in the body lock (extended from W07 passing + prior clinch work)**: Secure inside wrist or collar tie early; keep chest-to-chest or hip pressure so they cannot frame or separate. The lock is a control, not just a grip.
- **Immediate side mount dominance on landing (frame + posture)**: Knee in hip or thigh, far hip/thigh block or underhook, head up or controlling their head/shoulder. Do not land and pause — establish the position you fought to reach.
- **Push kick as timing/disruption tool, not just strike (GB curriculum self-defense context)**: The teep freezes or pushes to create the exact window for the level change and lock. Range management is the setup, not the finish.
- **Consistent self-defense takedown language across Week 2/3/4/7 (Harrogate FXW6LE_6wQ4 + Scottsdale + Centennial)**: Chin tuck, V-grip/wrist control, block hip + frame/drive, technical transitions, heavy base. All GB affiliates reinforce the same postural and pressure principles.

## Media & Visual References

**GB Affiliate / Curriculum Priority (per GB Greensboro rule):**
- Primary curriculum anchor: https://gbcentennial.com/week-7/ and https://gbcentennial.com/week-7-leg-grabs-and-passing-guard-mastery-at-gracie-barra/ (GB Centennial) — Deep reviewed for exact GB1 Week 7 'distance management + body lock takedown into side mount' and 'double leg turning the corner' / inside hook trip mechanics.
- Foundational transfer: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FXW6LE_6wQ4 (GB Harrogate Week 2) — Deep reviewed for self-defense entry, wrist/hip control, frame/drive, and technical mount mechanics that directly support distance entries and bodylock clinch work.
- Cross-ref to W03-A1 jab slip double leg (Scottsdale/Harrogate) for level change + hip drive consistency already enriched in prior waves.

See the structured videos[] frontmatter above for real GB affiliate embeds with exact curriculum and mechanics details. These are the highest-fidelity public sources for this GB1 Week 7 distance/takedown sequence.

Cross-references: Drill the full W07 distance family (A1 + B1) together, then connect to W08-B1 bodylock clinch extensions and the W07 guard passing cluster (A2/A3) once side mount is secured. Also flows from W02/W03/W04 self-defense takedown/escape work.

**Recommended Photos / Diagrams**
- Push kick (front teep) to hip creating the freeze/entry window.
- Level change (hips back, knees bent, head up/outside) into body lock or double leg.
- Body lock grip (waist/high clinch) with inside wrist control and chest/hip pressure.
- Double leg finish: turning the corner (45-90° hip angle) or inside hook trip.
- Side mount landing: knee in hip, far hip/thigh frame, head/posture control, heavy base.
- Contrast: good head position vs exposed neck during drive.

**Hand Placement & References**  
Push kick target (hip/midsection for disruption), body lock with inside wrist/collar tie + chest-to-chest or hip pressure, level change with hips/knees (head up/outside), hip drive + weight through base/legs, corner turn or inside hook for the leg trip, and immediate side mount frame/posture on landing — all drawn from the deeply reviewed GB Centennial Week 7 curriculum pages plus consistent Harrogate/Scottsdale self-defense mechanics (under the standing GB Greensboro priority). This is the definitive distance management + body lock / double leg entry for GB1 Week 7.

## Personal Cues & Notes

GB1-W07-B1 - Distance Mgmt + Jab + Cross.body:
## Concept

**Position:** Standing / Distance Management
**Category:** Takedown / Self-Defense
**Curriculum:** GB1 Week 7 B1
**Confidence:** 4/5

From distance, use jab-cross combinations to set up level change entries into the double leg takedown (turning the corner or inside hook trip variations), landing in side mount or advancing to mount. Distance management and timing on the strikes create the window; the double leg mechanics (angle, hook, drive) finish the sequence. Core GB1 Week 7 stand-up self-defense and sport takedown.

## Setup

You are at kicking/striking distance. Opponent is postured or circling.

- Goal: Use jab-cross to disrupt hands/head and create a momentary freeze or posture break, then level change into double leg (turn the corner or inside hook the posting leg), drive through with hip weight, and finish to side mount or better.
- Direct extension of W03-A1 (jab slip double leg) and W04-A1 (outside hook takedown) with the explicit Week 7 curriculum emphasis on distance management + double leg variations.

Wikilink connections:
- Part of Week 7 distance family: see [[GB1-W07-A1 - Distance Mgmt + Front Push Kick + Bodylock Takedown + Side Mount]], [[GB1-W08-B1 - Jab Slip + Bodylock Clinch + Rear Bodylock + Wrist Control + Forward Takedown]].
- Builds directly on [[GB1-W03-A1 - Jab Slip to Double Leg Takedown]] (same Harrogate/Scottsdale level change + hip drive + head position mechanics now contextualized in Week 7 curriculum).
- Connects to W08-B1 bodylock clinch extensions and W07 guard passing arrivals in side mount.
- Cross-ref to [[GB1-W02-B3 - Double Leg Takedown from Turtle Position]] and W06 recoveries for entry/exit consistency.

## Execution

### Jab-Cross Setup + Timing Window
1. Active hands and posture at distance; do not reach or lean.
2. Jab (or jab-cross combination) to the head/hands to occupy their defense and create a split-second posture or balance disruption.
3. As the cross lands or is defended, immediately drop level (hips back, knees bent) and shoot the double leg entry.

### Double Leg Entry + Finish (Turning the Corner or Inside Hook Trip)
1. Level change with head up/outside or tight to their hip/leg line (protect the neck).
2. Secure the legs (both hands behind or one high/one low as per variation); drive forward with hip elevation and weight through your base.
3. Finish options per GB Centennial Week 7: turn your hips 45-90° ("turning the corner") to angle past their base, or shoot an inside hook on their posting leg as you drive through.
4. Land heavy in side mount (or advance to mount if the angle allows), with immediate knee in hip/thigh, far hip frame or underhook, and head/posture control.

### Hand Placement & References
- **Jab-cross target (head/hands)**: Occupies defense and creates the timing freeze — not a power strike, a setup.
- **Level change (hips/knees, head up/outside)**: The critical entry motor. Shoulders do not lead.
- **Double leg grip (behind both legs or high/low)**: Secure before the drive; do not reach with the head.
- **Corner turn or inside hook trip**: The angle or leg trip that finishes even against good sprawl defense (GB Centennial explicit).
- **Hip drive + weight through base/legs**: Forward pressure + slight elevation; heavy on the finish, not floating.
- **Side mount arrival frame/posture**: Immediate dominance (knee line, far hip block, head control).

## Common Mistakes
- Striking without the immediate level change (opponent recovers posture and sprawls).
- Level change with shoulders/head first (guillotine or sprawl exposure).
- No angle or hook on the finish (straight drive gets stuffed).
- Weak leg grip or no hip drive (they separate or you end up in a bad scramble).
- Landing without immediate frame/posture (they bridge or recover guard).
- Losing head position during the shot (neck exposed).

## Key Principles
- **Distance management + double leg turning the corner / inside hook trip (GB Centennial Week 7 curriculum)**: The explicit GB1 Week 7 focus — jab-cross as setup, then double leg with angle (turn the corner) or inside hook trip to finish. "Double leg turning the corner technique" and "double leg takedown with an inside hook trip."
- **Jab-cross as timing/disruption, not power (extended from W03-A1)**: The strikes occupy hands/head to create the exact window for the level change. Consistent with Harrogate/Scottsdale self-defense entry mechanics.
- **Level change + hip drive with head up/outside (W03-A1 Harrogate/Scottsdale + W02)**: Hips back and knees bent; head position protects and maintains posture throughout the shot and drive. "Level change + hip drive (not shoulders); head up/outside during drive."
- **Corner turn or inside hook as the finisher (GB Centennial Week 7)**: Do not drive straight into a good base. Angle your hips or hook the inside leg to collapse their base even when they sprawl or post.
- **Heavy landing + immediate side mount control (consistent across W02/W03/W06/W07)**: Knee in hip, far hip/thigh frame or underhook, head up or controlling their posture. The takedown is only as good as the position you land in.
- **Wrist/hip control transfer from clinch to leg attack (W07 family + prior body lock work)**: If the entry meets resistance, the same wrist control and hip pressure principles from body lock and guard passing apply to stay connected.
- **Consistent GB self-defense takedown language (Harrogate Week 2 + Scottsdale + Centennial Week 7)**: Chin tuck/posture, level change not shoulders, hip drive, heavy top, technical finish. All GB affiliates reinforce the same principles under the standing priority.

## Media & Visual References

**GB Affiliate / Curriculum Priority (per GB Greensboro rule):**
- Primary: https://gbcentennial.com/week-7/ and https://gbcentennial.com/week-7-leg-grabs-and-passing-guard-mastery-at-gracie-barra/ (GB Centennial) — Deep reviewed for exact GB1 Week 7 'distance management into a double leg takedown', 'double leg turning the corner', and 'inside hook trip' mechanics in Fundamentals and advanced classes.
- Foundational transfer: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FXW6LE_6wQ4 (GB Harrogate Week 2) — Deep reviewed for the jab slip + level change + hip drive + head position mechanics that are the direct precursor to this Week 7 double leg variation.
- Cross-ref to W03-A1 (Scottsdale/Harrogate) for the same entry mechanics already enriched with GB affiliate deep review.

See the structured videos[] frontmatter above for real GB affiliate embeds with exact curriculum and mechanics details. These are the highest-fidelity public sources for this GB1 Week 7 jab-cross + double leg sequence.

Cross-references: Drill the full W07 distance family (A1 push kick bodylock + B1 jab-cross double leg) together. Connect to W08-B1 bodylock clinch and W07 guard passing (A2/A3) once side mount is secured. Also flows from W02/W03/W04 self-defense takedown work.

**Recommended Photos / Diagrams**
- Jab-cross setup at distance (head/hands occupation).
- Level change (hips back, knees bent, head up/outside) into double leg.
- Double leg finish: turning the corner (hip angle) or inside hook trip on posting leg.
- Heavy side mount landing with knee in hip, far hip frame, head/posture.
- Contrast: straight drive (stuffed) vs corner/angle finish (successful).

**Hand Placement & References**  
Jab-cross for timing (head/hands), level change with hips/knees (head up/outside), secure leg grip before drive, corner turn or inside hook as finisher, hip drive + weight through base, and immediate side mount frame/posture on landing — all from the deeply reviewed GB Centennial Week 7 curriculum plus consistent Harrogate/Scottsdale self-defense mechanics (under the standing GB Greensboro priority). This is the definitive jab-cross + double leg entry for GB1 Week 7.

## Personal Cues & Notes

GB1-W08-B1 - Jab Slip + Bodylock Clinch.body:
## Concept

**Position:** Standing / Clinch
**Category:** Takedown / Self-Defense
**Curriculum:** GB1 Week 8 B1
**Confidence:** 4/5

Jab slip entry into body lock clinch (high or waist), secure rear body lock with wrist control, and drive a forward takedown (or trip) while maintaining the lock and posture. The slip creates the angle; the rear body lock + wrist control provides the control and drive. Natural Week 8 extension of Week 7 distance/body lock work and prior self-defense clinch/takedown entries.

## Setup

You are at striking distance or closing. Opponent throws a jab or advances.

- Goal: Slip the jab (head movement + angle), immediately secure body lock clinch (high or waist), transition to rear body lock with strong wrist control, and drive forward or trip to takedown while keeping chest/hip pressure and posture.
- Builds on W07-A1/B1 distance entries and W03-A1/W04-A1 slip/hook takedowns; connects to W08 mount attacks and guard passing arrivals.

Wikilink connections:
- Part of Week 7/8 distance/clinch/takedown family: see [[GB1-W07-A1 - Distance Mgmt + Front Push Kick + Bodylock Takedown + Side Mount]], [[GB1-W07-B1 - Distance Mgmt + Jab + Cross + Double Leg]], [[GB1-W08-A1 - Block Hook Punches + Headlock Takedown + Armbar]].
- Direct extension of [[GB1-W03-A1 - Jab Slip to Double Leg Takedown]] (same slip + level/angle + hip drive) and [[GB1-W02-B1 - Escape Rear Headlock + Forward Takedown - Outside Hook Takedown]].
- Leads into W08 mount transitions/attacks (A2/B2/A3) once top position is secured.

## Execution

### Jab Slip + Immediate Clinch Entry
1. Active posture and hands; do not reach or lean into the jab.
2. Slip the jab (head movement to the outside or inside angle) while stepping or angling to create the entry.
3. Immediately wrap the body lock (high clinch under the arms or waist) with chest-to-chest or hip pressure.

### Rear Body Lock + Wrist Control
1. Transition or adjust to rear body lock (behind their back or high under the arms) while maintaining the angle from the slip.
2. Secure inside wrist control or collar tie on the far side to prevent their frame or underhook.
3. Keep head position tight to their shoulder/hip or up/outside; chest/hip pressure so they cannot separate.

### Forward Takedown Drive or Trip
1. Drive forward with hip elevation and weight through your base/legs (not floating).
2. Use the rear lock + wrist control to lift or turn their hips; add a trip (inside or outside leg) as needed.
3. Land in side mount or mount with immediate frame, knee line, and posture control (do not pause on landing).

### Hand Placement & References
- **Jab slip (head movement + angle step)**: Creates the entry without absorbing the strike.
- **Body lock clinch (high or waist + chest/hip pressure)**: The bridge from slip to rear lock.
- **Rear body lock + inside wrist/collar tie control**: The dominant grip that prevents separation and allows the drive/lift.
- **Hip drive + weight through base during forward finish**: Forward pressure + slight elevation; do not lean or overcommit the head.
- **Trip (inside/outside leg) as needed**: Augments the lock when they post or base.
- **Side/mount arrival (knee in hip, far hip frame, head/posture)**: Immediate dominance.

## Common Mistakes
- Slipping but pausing before the clinch entry (opponent resets posture).
- Weak body lock (arms only, no chest/hip connection) — they frame and separate.
- Losing wrist control in the rear lock (they underhook or elbow frame).
- No hip drive or floating weight on the finish (they sprawl or spin out).
- Landing without immediate frame/posture (they recover guard or bridge you off).
- Head position too low or forward during drive (neck exposed or you get thrown).

## Key Principles
- **Jab slip + immediate body lock clinch entry (extended from W03-A1 + W07 distance work)**: The slip creates the angle; do not pause — wrap the lock while the window is open. Same timing principle as the jab slip double leg in W03-A1.
- **Rear body lock + wrist control as the control mechanism (GB Centennial Week 7 body lock principles + Harrogate clinch)**: Inside wrist or collar tie prevents their frame/underhook. The lock is a control position, not just a grip. Chest/hip pressure keeps you connected.
- **Forward drive with hip elevation + weight through base (consistent GB posture/hip drive from W03/W04/W06/W07)**: Hips and legs do the work; head stays up or tight. "Hip drive + weight through base/legs."
- **Trip augmentation when they base (GB Centennial leg grab/double leg context)**: Use inside or outside hook/trip to collapse the remaining base when the pure drive meets resistance.
- **Immediate top position dominance on landing (W07/W08 family + prior takedowns)**: Knee in hip, far hip/thigh frame or underhook, head/posture control. The takedown is only valuable if you own the position you land in.
- **Consistent self-defense clinch/takedown language (Harrogate Week 2 + Scottsdale + Centennial)**: Wrist control, hip pressure, frame/drive instead of strength, technical transitions, heavy base. All GB affiliates reinforce the same principles.
- **Progression from Week 7 distance/body lock to Week 8 clinch/rear lock entries (GB curriculum flow)**: The same postural, wrist, and hip pressure mechanics scale from open distance entries to clinch and rear body lock finishes.

## Media & Visual References

**GB Affiliate / Curriculum Priority (per GB Greensboro rule):**
- Primary curriculum: https://gbcentennial.com/week-7/ (GB Centennial) — Deep reviewed for body lock takedown and distance management mechanics that underpin the clinch and rear body lock variations.
- Foundational transfer: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FXW6LE_6wQ4 (GB Harrogate Week 2) — Deep reviewed for the clinch control, wrist/hip grip, frame/drive, and safe transition mechanics that directly support jab slip into bodylock clinch + rear body lock + forward takedown.
- Week 8 context: https://gbcentennial.com/week-8/ (GB Centennial) — Curriculum progression for clinch and mount attack entries building on Week 7 foundations.
- Cross-ref to W03-A1 and W04-A1 (Scottsdale/Harrogate) for the slip + level/hip drive consistency already enriched.

See the structured videos[] frontmatter above for real GB affiliate embeds with exact mechanics details. These are the highest-fidelity public sources for this GB1 Week 8 clinch/rear body lock forward takedown.

Cross-references: Drill the full W07/W08 distance/clinch/takedown family together (A1 push kick bodylock, B1 jab-cross double leg, B1 jab slip rear bodylock). Connect to W08 mount work (A2/B2/A3) and W07 guard passing once top position is secured. Also flows from W02/W03/W04 self-defense clinch/takedown work.

**Recommended Photos / Diagrams**
- Jab slip (head movement + angle) into body lock clinch wrap.
- Rear body lock with inside wrist/collar tie control and chest/hip pressure.
- Forward drive with hip elevation and base weight; inside/outside trip augmentation.
- Side or mount landing with immediate knee line, far hip frame, head/posture.
- Contrast: weak lock (separated) vs strong wrist/hip pressure (connected drive).

**Hand Placement & References**  
Jab slip for angle, body lock clinch bridge (high or waist + chest/hip), rear body lock + inside wrist/collar tie control, hip drive + weight through base/legs on forward finish, trip augmentation as needed, and immediate top position frame/posture on landing — drawn from the deeply reviewed GB Centennial Week 7/8 curriculum pages plus consistent Harrogate/Scottsdale self-defense clinch and takedown mechanics (under the standing GB Greensboro priority). This is the definitive jab slip + rear body lock forward takedown for GB1 Week 8.

GB1-W08-A1 - Block Hook Punches + Headlo.body:
## Concept

**Position:** Standing / Self-Defense
**Category:** Self-Defense
**Curriculum:** GB1 Week 8 A1
**Confidence:** 4/5

Defend hook punches (high guard or specific blocks), secure the headlock (or convert opponent's headlock), and finish with a takedown (outside hook or forward) directly into technical mount or side mount + armbar. The defense (chin tuck, frames, posture) creates the opportunity; the takedown + armbar finishes the threat. Core GB1 Week 8 self-defense progression from Week 2 headlock work.

## Setup

You are standing. Opponent throws hook punches or secures a headlock (side or rear).

- Goal: Block or frame the hook punches while protecting the neck (chin tuck), secure or convert the headlock position, execute the takedown (outside hook or forward drive), and finish in technical mount or side with armbar.
- Direct extension of W02-A1 (side standing headlock + rear takedown + technical mount + armbar) and W02-B1 (rear headlock + forward/outside hook takedown) with Week 8 hook punch defense integration.

Wikilink connections:
- Part of Week 8 self-defense/headlock family: see [[GB1-W08-B1 - Jab Slip + Bodylock Clinch + Rear Bodylock + Wrist Control + Forward Takedown]], [[GB1-W08-B3 - Spinning Armbar]].
- Builds on [[GB1-W02-A1 - Escape Side Standing Headlock + Rear Takedown + Technical Mount + Armbar]] and [[GB1-W02-B1 - Escape Rear Headlock + Forward Takedown - Outside Hook Takedown]] (exact Harrogate mechanics now with hook punch defense layer).
- Connects to W06 headlock escape to back/shoulder lock (B1) and W04 mount headlock escape (A2) for the defensive side.
- Leads into W08 mount attacks (A3 papercutter) once top position + armbar threat is established.

## Execution

### Hook Punch Defense + Posture (Chin Tuck + Frames)
1. High guard or specific hook blocks (forearm/elbow up, hands active) while maintaining chin tucked and neck protected.
2. Do not reach or lean; use footwork and posture to manage the range.
3. If headlock is secured (side or rear): immediate chin tuck to protect the neck, stiff frame with the inside arm, and posture break or base creation.

### Headlock Conversion + Takedown Entry
1. Use the frame + posture to create space or angle (reverse lunge or hip block as per Harrogate mechanics).
2. Secure the headlock (if converting opponent's) or maintain control while setting up the takedown (outside hook or forward drive).
3. Drive with hip elevation and weight through base; use the headlock grip + frames to off-balance and finish the trip or throw.

### Takedown Finish + Armbar
1. Land in technical mount or side mount with immediate posture and base.
2. From technical mount: isolate the arm (wrist control or elbow trap) and finish the armbar with hip drive and angle (not strength).
3. Maintain head control or posture throughout to prevent their escape or reversal.

### Hand Placement & References
- **Hook defense (high guard/forearm blocks + chin tuck)**: Protects the head/neck while occupying their striking arm.
- **Inside frame + stiff arm (not strength lift)**: Creates the space and posture break (Harrogate explicit: "frame/drive with back engagement + stiff frame (NOT strength lift)").
- **Headlock grip (thumb-down V-grip or collar tie)**: Control for the takedown drive or trip.
- **Outside hook or forward drive with hip elevation**: The finish motor (consistent with W02/W04 takedowns).
- **Technical mount arrival + arm isolation (wrist/elbow control)**: The submission setup.
- **Armbar finish (hip drive + angle, not strength)**: Leverage over strength, same principle as prior armbars in the curriculum.

## Common Mistakes
- Reaching or leaning into the hook punches (absorbs power or exposes the neck).
- No immediate chin tuck when headlock is secured (neck vulnerable to crank or choke).
- Weak frame (bending the arm or using strength instead of structure) — they collapse the posture.
- Dropping level or head too low during the takedown entry (guillotine or throw exposure).
- Landing without immediate technical mount posture (they bridge or recover guard).
- Armbar finish with strength instead of hip drive + angle (they escape or reverse).

## Key Principles
- **Immediate chin tuck + neck protection on any headlock threat (GB Harrogate Week 2 explicit)**: "Immediate chin tuck/neck protect" is non-negotiable. Do not wait to feel the pressure.
- **Stiff frame + drive with back engagement, not raw strength (Harrogate FXW6LE_6wQ4)**: "Frame/drive with back engagement + stiff frame (NOT strength lift)." The structure does the work; your back and posture create the space and off-balance.
- **Hook punch defense as posture + range management (Week 8 self-defense extension)**: High guard/blocks + footwork and chin tuck while staying calm. The defense itself creates the opportunity for the headlock conversion or takedown entry.
- **Headlock grip + hip drive/trip finish (consistent GB self-defense from W02/W04)**: Use the headlock as control for the outside hook or forward drive. "Block hip + open shoulder, frame/drive, technical mount transition."
- **Technical mount + arm isolation for armbar (Harrogate + W02/W06)**: Land with posture, then isolate the arm with wrist/elbow control before finishing with hip drive and angle. Leverage over strength.
- **Consistent self-defense headlock/takedown language across Week 2/4/6/8 (Harrogate + Scottsdale + Centennial)**: Chin tuck, V-grip/wrist control, frame/drive not strength, technical transitions, heavy base, armbar as the finish. All GB affiliates reinforce the same principles under the standing priority.
- **Progression from Week 2 headlock escapes to Week 8 hook defense + headlock takedown (GB curriculum flow)**: The same postural and pressure mechanics scale from pure defense to offensive conversion and finish.

## Media & Visual References

**GB Affiliate / Curriculum Priority (per GB Greensboro rule):**
- Primary foundational: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FXW6LE_6wQ4 (GB Harrogate Week 2) — Deep reviewed for the exact side/rear headlock defense mechanics (chin tuck, V-grip, reverse lunge, block hip, frame/drive with back engagement, technical mount + armbar) that are the direct base for Week 8 hook punch defense + headlock takedown conversion.
- Curriculum context: https://gbcentennial.com/week-7/ and https://gbcentennial.com/week-8/ (GB Centennial) — Deep reviewed for GB1 self-defense distance/takedown and Week 8 progression that frames the hook defense + headlock work.
- Cross-ref to W02-A1/B1 and W04-A1 (Harrogate/Scottsdale) for the same self-defense clinch/takedown mechanics already enriched with GB affiliate deep review.

See the structured videos[] frontmatter above for real GB affiliate embeds with exact defense and finish details. These are the highest-fidelity public sources for this GB1 Week 8 hook punch + headlock takedown + armbar sequence.

Cross-references: Drill the full W08 self-defense headlock family (A1 hook defense + headlock takedown + armbar, B1 clinch bodylock forward takedown) together. Connect to W06 headlock escape to back (B1) and W04 mount headlock escape (A2) for the defensive side. Also flows from W02 headlock work and into W08 mount attacks (A3 papercutter).

**Recommended Photos / Diagrams**
- High guard or forearm blocks vs hook punches with chin tucked.
- Side or rear headlock with immediate chin tuck + inside stiff frame.
- Reverse lunge or hip block + frame/drive posture break (Harrogate mechanics).
- Outside hook or forward drive takedown with headlock control.
- Technical mount landing + wrist/elbow isolation for armbar finish (hip drive + angle).
- Contrast: chin up/exposed vs chin tucked/protected.

**Hand Placement & References**  
Hook defense (high guard/forearm blocks + chin tuck), inside stiff frame + back engagement for posture break (not strength), headlock grip (thumb-down V-grip or collar tie), outside hook or forward drive with hip elevation, technical mount arrival with posture, and wrist/elbow control + hip drive/angle for the armbar finish — all from the deeply reviewed GB Harrogate Week 2 curriculum (explicit self-defense mechanics) plus consistent Scottsdale/Centennial extensions (under the standing GB Greensboro priority). This is the definitive hook punch defense + headlock takedown + armbar for GB1 Week 8.

## Personal Cues & Notes

GB1-W08-B3 - Spinning Armbar.md.body:
## Concept

**Position:** Mount / Side Control
**Category:** Submission
**Curriculum:** GB1 Week 8 B3
**Confidence:** 4/5

From mount or strong side control, use a spinning entry (hip switch or leg over) to isolate the arm and finish the armbar with hip drive, angle, and leverage (not strength). The spin creates the isolation; the finish uses the same postural and hip drive principles as other mount attacks (papercutter, etc.). High-percentage GB1 Week 8 mount offense.

## Setup

You have mount or strong side control. Opponent is defending or attempting to bridge/escape.

- Goal: Use a controlled spin or leg switch to isolate one arm, secure wrist/elbow control, and finish the armbar with hip drive and proper angle while maintaining base and heavy pressure.
- Direct follow-up to W08 mount transitions (A2 switch base, B2 sliding foot) and guard passes that arrive in mount (W07-A3 half-guard hook, W03-B3 knee slide). Also connects to W08-A3 papercutter as alternative mount attack.

Wikilink connections:
- Part of Week 8 mount attack family: see [[GB1-W08-A3 - Papercutter Choke]], [[GB1-W08-A2 - Transition to Mount (Switch Base)]], [[GB1-W08-B2 - Transition to Mount Sliding Foot Over Belly]].
- Builds on [[GB1-W07-A3 - Half-Guard Pass with Hook + Hip Elevation + Mount]] and [[GB1-W06-B2 - Bridge Escape from the Back + Side Mount]] for arrival and control.
- Cross-ref to [[GB1-W04-A2 - Escape Mount with Headlock Using Bridge]] for the defensive side of the position.
- Armbar finish mechanics consistent with [[GB1-W08-A1 - Block Hook Punches + Headlock Takedown + Armbar]] and Harrogate technical mount armbar.

## Execution

### Spin Entry + Arm Isolation (Hip Switch or Leg Over)
1. From mount or side, maintain heavy base and posture (do not float).
2. Use a hip switch or controlled leg over (spin) to create the angle and isolate the target arm (far arm or near arm depending on grip).
3. Secure the wrist (thumb down or as per grip) and elbow control (triceps or forearm) while the spin completes.

### Armbar Finish (Hip Drive + Angle + Leverage)
1. Extend the arm with wrist control; keep your hips close and heavy on their chest/shoulder.
2. Drive your hips forward or up (depending on exact variation) while creating the angle (your body perpendicular or 45° to theirs).
3. Finish with hip drive and leverage (not pulling with the arms or using strength). Maintain base with the free hand or knee.
4. If they roll or bridge, use the momentum or adjust the angle to stay on top and finish.

### Hand Placement & References
- **Wrist control (thumb down or grip variation)**: The primary isolation; prevents them from bending the arm or rolling out.
- **Elbow control (triceps/forearm)**: Keeps the arm straight and creates the fulcrum.
- **Hips close + heavy on chest/shoulder during spin and finish**: The pressure that makes the armbar tight and prevents escape.
- **Hip drive + angle (body perpendicular or 45°)**: The motor of the finish — leverage, not strength.
- **Base maintenance (free hand or knee posting)**: Do not sacrifice position for the submission.

## Common Mistakes
- Spinning too fast or loose (lose base or they roll with you and reverse).
- Weak wrist control (they bend the arm or roll the shoulder out).
- Hips too far away or floating (no pressure, easy escape).
- Finishing with arm strength instead of hip drive + angle (they defend or reverse).
- Losing mount/side position while attacking the armbar (they escape to guard or turtle).
- No adjustment when they roll/bridge (you get rolled or lose the arm).

## Key Principles
- **Spinning entry with base and posture (GB curriculum + consistent W08 mount attacks)**: The spin is controlled (hip switch or leg over), not a wild roll. Maintain heavy base and posture throughout — attacking the armbar does not mean giving up position (same principle as papercutter from W08-A3).
- **Wrist + elbow control before the finish (Harrogate technical mount armbar + W08-A1)**: Secure the wrist (thumb down) and elbow control first. The isolation is what makes the armbar work; the spin just creates the angle.
- **Hips close + heavy pressure on chest/shoulder (GB HQ papercutter pressure + W08 mount family)**: "Heavy weight, wide base, upright posture. Attacking the submission does not mean giving up position." The pressure makes the armbar tight and prevents bridges.
- **Hip drive + angle, not strength (consistent GB armbar language from Harrogate + W08-A1)**: "Leverage over strength." Drive your hips and create the perpendicular or 45° angle; do not pull with the arms.
- **Base maintenance during the finish (W08 mount attacks + prior mount work)**: Free hand or knee posts for base. If they roll or bridge, use the momentum or adjust without losing the top position.
- **Consistent mount attack language across Week 8 (papercutter + spinning armbar + transitions)**: Posture, weight through base, hip drive elements, grip specifics, and heavy pressure tie all W08 mount attacks together (A2/B2 entries, A3 papercutter, B3 spinning armbar).
- **Progression from Week 7 guard passing/mount arrivals to Week 8 mount offense (GB curriculum flow)**: The same postural and pressure principles that got you to mount now finish the attack from mount.

## Media & Visual References

**GB Affiliate / Curriculum Priority (per GB Greensboro rule):**
- Primary curriculum: https://gbcentennial.com/week-8/ (GB Centennial) — Deep reviewed for Week 8 mount attack context (papercutter, spinning armbar, transitions) building on Week 7 guard passing and mount entries.
- Pressure/finish reference: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HPgp49nk7vY (GB HQ) — Deep reviewed for mount/side attack pressure (heavy chest/shoulder weight, elbow drive, base maintenance) that transfers directly to spinning armbar entries and finishes.
- Armbar mechanics transfer: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FXW6LE_6wQ4 (GB Harrogate Week 2) — Deep reviewed for the technical mount armbar finish (wrist/elbow isolation + hip drive + angle, not strength) used in the spinning variation.
- Cross-ref to W08-A3 papercutter (HQ + Aliso + gbcentennial) for the same mount attack postural and pressure principles already enriched.

See the structured videos[] frontmatter above for real GB affiliate embeds with exact pressure, grip, and finish details. These are the highest-fidelity public sources for this GB1 Week 8 spinning armbar from mount/side.

Cross-references: Drill the full W08 mount attack family (A3 papercutter, B3 spinning armbar) together after arriving via W08 mount transitions (A2/B2) or W07 guard passes (A3). Also connects to W06 side mount arrivals and W04 mount escapes for the attack-defense loop.

**Recommended Photos / Diagrams**
- Spinning entry (hip switch or leg over) from mount with base maintained.
- Wrist control (thumb down) + elbow control during the spin.
- Hips close and heavy on chest/shoulder during the finish.
- Hip drive + body angle (perpendicular or 45°) for the armbar.
- Base maintenance (free hand or knee) throughout.
- Contrast: hips floating (easy escape) vs hips heavy + driving (tight finish).

**Hand Placement & References**  
Wrist control (thumb down) + elbow control for isolation, hips close + heavy on chest/shoulder during spin and finish, hip drive + angle (not strength) for the armbar, and base maintenance (free hand or knee) throughout — drawn from the deeply reviewed GB Centennial Week 8 curriculum, GB HQ mount attack pressure (papercutter transfer), and consistent Harrogate technical mount armbar mechanics (under the standing GB Greensboro priority). This is the definitive spinning armbar for GB1 Week 8.

## Personal Cues & Notes
